questions.question: What is overfitting and how do you prevent it?
questions.answer: Overfitting: the model memorizes the training data (including noise) instead of learning generalizable patterns. It performs great on training data but poorly on new data. Prevention: (1) More training data. (2) Regularization (L1/Lasso, L2/Ridge) — penalize large weights. (3) Dropout (neural networks) — randomly zero out neurons during training. (4) Cross-validation — evaluate on multiple held-out sets. (5) Simpler model — fewer parameters. (6) Early stopping — stop training when validation loss starts increasing. (7) Data augmentation — generate more training examples from existing ones.

questions.question: What is the bias-variance tradeoff?
questions.answer: Bias: error from wrong assumptions — the model is too simple and misses patterns (underfitting). High bias = low accuracy on training AND test data. Variance: sensitivity to training data fluctuations — the model is too complex and learns noise (overfitting). High variance = high accuracy on training, low on test. The tradeoff: reducing bias (more complexity) increases variance, and vice versa. Goal: find the "sweet spot" with low bias AND low variance. Ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) explicitly manage this tradeoff.

questions.question: What is the difference between precision and recall?
questions.answer: Precision = true positives / (true positives + false positives). "Of all the positives I predicted, how many were actually positive?" Recall = true positives / (true positives + false negatives). "Of all the actual positives, how many did I catch?" Trade-off example: Cancer detection — prefer high RECALL (catch all cancers, even if some false alarms). Spam filter — prefer high PRECISION (every email in spam folder IS spam, even if some spam slips through to inbox). F1 score = harmonic mean of precision and recall — a balanced metric.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Machine Learning Core Principles Part 2A queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
